problems.summary: User recently had a poor presentation experience at a conference with a low feedback score, and is anxious about an upcoming review and potential team restructuring. They feel their self-worth is tied to their performance metric, which is causing exhaustion. The user is replaying the presentation in their head, worried about their competence and standing in the review, feeling like their career is at a pivot point and unsure how to move forward. They can't recall a time when they felt so pressured before and are stuck in a loop of perfection and self-doubt, still replaying a similar stumble from last year. The user has realized that their fear of being magnified by their superiors leads them to set impossibly high standards, causing them to replay every detail to prove themselves flawless, which feels exhausting. The user is now questioning the catastrophic nature of not meeting these standards, but still feels trapped by the thought that their value is tied to this one presentation, making the upcoming review feel life-changing.

parsed.reasoning: I'm a supportive companion to this person right now, and they need help deliberating a decision about their work stress. user is questioning the catastrophic nature of not meeting impossibly high standards

parsed.final_response: You're still grappling with the fear that not meeting those high standards will make you seem incompetent, but at the same time, you're starting to question whether the fallout would really be as bad as you think. It's like you're caught between the desire to prove yourself flawless and the realization that maybe, just maybe, it's not the end of the world if you don't hit those standards. The fact that you're tying your self-worth to this one presentation is a heavy burden, and it's making the upcoming review feel like a make-or-break moment. Can you imagine what would happen if you were able to separate your self-worth from this one metric, even just a little bit?
